sentences.text: You are going to say that?
My name is so on.
I can do it if-- you want me to do it?
But, name-- that my name is so and so and I live at this and this address .
Is that what you want?
I wouldn't give the address.
Just the city.
OK, and the date.
Today is the 11th?
OK, fine.
Then you start.
I was born in Miskolc, Hungary, which is Eastern Europe, 66 years ago.
My mother described the day.
It was very beautiful.
It was May 1, and she delivered me without any help.
Miskolc was a town of about 60,000 people, 10,000 of them were Jews.
We had a large Orthodox community as well a Neolog community.

My father belonged to a smaller section of the Orthodox and they were called [? fardim ?] The way-- the reason they were called [? fardim ?] because they divined, they prayed in a different order than the rest of them.
Also they were more observant.
Most of the people in Miskolc, although they called themselves Orthodox, they really did not keep the precepts so carefully.
My family did.
I grew up in a very traditional family.
I had an older brother, whose name was Zoltan, and we called him Yitzhak, and a younger brother whose name was Tibor and we called him Moshe.
My father's name was Jeno, or Yakov Koppel in Yiddish.
He was a man of great integrity.
He lost most of his possessions during the Depression.
And, slowly he built himself up with the help of my mother and they owned a kosher restaurant which was famous for its good food.
It was interesting that my mother tells me that she never learned how to cook when she was a child.
And, my grandfather gave her a cook when she got married.
And, so she was able to use this cook to open a restaurant with and maintain the family.
I went to a Jewish school.
Most kids went to some kind of religious school, either Catholic or, or Protestant or Jewish.
This was maintained by the community as well as by the state of Hungary.
The girls and the boys were separated.
I was in a class of 40 kids.
And, I was a very good student.
I never missed a day in school, and in order to understand that, I was probably too bored to stay home.
I didn't have toys or anything like that, so I rather went to school even when I was sick.
My childhood was pretty happy.
My parents worked very hard and they often sent me away to my grandparents to Edeleny, which was about 25 kilometers north of Miskolc.
My grandfather was a very important person in this town.
He owned many businesses, among them a coal mine, and he employed most of the town.
Now, I'm mentioning this because my grandfather played a very great role in my life.
He was the patriarch of the family.
My mother was one of 12 children and each of the children participated in one of the businesses there.
Unfortunately, when things became bad in Hungary, instead of being helped by the population, they turned against us.
Edeleny was a place where all the magic of my childhood took place.
And I-- I played happily, although often longed to back home.
My parents were so busy that they just didn't have time for me.
My grandfather, who was a self-made man-- he used to take me every morning on his horse and buggy to the coal mine and allowed me to go down with him and see how the miners work.
I don't know if this was important in terms of what I learned about mining, but it was important because he paid attention to me, and it was easy to get lost in a big family.
One of the things that we were told, when we ate dinner that we were not allowed to speak.
And, so when we wanted something we had to cough and that's how we got some attention.
In Miskolc, where I went to school, I had many friends.
Didn't have any non-Jewish friends.
Most of my friends were Jewish.
I had a neighbor who was not Jewish, who was a boy.
And, in the beginning, they allowed him to play with me.
But, again, when the anti-Semitism became worse, he was no longer allowed to play with me.
I remember seeing, with longing, through the window, their Christmas tree, and how much I would have wanted to be invited in, but I wasn't.
There was a, a friend-- there was a friend of mine who played with other children in the courtyard and I was with her.
And, I remember she asked this girl to lunch.
She was also a non-Jewish girl.
And, the mother instructed her daughter, you can go to lunch but take the crust of the bread because it was touched by the Jews.
Now, these are the things that I remember as a child.
How we were not like other people.
I remember when I was little and we heard about Hitler, how we used to make plans how to get to rid of him.
Little children, how we would trick him into going someplace where he would fall down and break his neck and all kinds of things.
Now, we heard most of the news through the shul where my, my father went.
Because our newspapers really did not report everything.
They were always censored.
And, I remember being scared that there will be another war.
I remember my parents talking about it, and I remember praying-- I was very religious as a little child-- praying that there should not be another war.
My parents were so busy that they did not have a lot of time for me.
I spent most of my time with the maid, who took care of me because my parents were so busy.
And, she was not Jewish and somehow, we worked out a, a wonderful relationship with my-- she even learned how to say the prayers with me in Hebrew, so that she really substituted my mother.
My two brothers went to Chedar and in the later years, in the late '30s, early '40s, they could not go alone anymore because they were stoned by the Gentile kids.
So that my-- the maid had to accompany them, and they often came back with, with bloody heads after they were hit.
There were many nights when our business, which had an iron, like, shutter from the outside, which we closed every night that people were banging on them.
And, ask them, open you-- open it up, you dirty Jews.
And I remember how scared I was and seeing my mother grabbing a kitchen knife and going toward the door.
Of course, they could not break in because the iron shutters kept them out, but I remember seeing my mother with a kitchen knife.
It was a, a terrible sight, and I knew something was terribly wrong.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My brother went to, to yeshiva after his bar mitzvah and I, I longed to go also.
I was the middle child and I was a girl, and of course that wasn't allowed for me to do.
I always wanted to be a scholar and I was somewhat of, of a tomboy.
But, because I could not go to study more, I developed my athletic abilities.
And, in school I became the President of the Athletic Club and the Literary Club.
It gave me a lot of status and a lot of good feeling about myself.
So, in spite of the, the environment, which was so negative and which was so harmful, I had a lot of positives coming to me through my efforts at school and through the friends that I enjoyed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- when I was born, I was born with a dislocated hip and I was told to exercise a lot when that was put in place.
And, so I went to ice skating and all kinds of other activities.

The rabbi of our congregation did not like that, that I mingled so freely with other children, especially with those that were not observant or they were not Jewish and, told my mother not to allow me.
And I remember my mother standing up for me and saying to, to the religious authority, in these decisions, I have to decide, and that she has to do the exercises, therefore, she will be going.
And, so this is the kind of model my mother was.
Not so much the nurturing and understanding person, but the one that always stood up, that always was there when there was a problem, and she was very courageous and strong.
My father was somewhat different.
My father was a milder person, more gentle.

And, I remember when my mother and father had an argument about how much to charge in the restaurant, my father always said just as much as we need and a little bit after that, but not more than that because we cannot take advantage of people.
And, my mother, who came from a business family, who always helped her father, could not understand that.
How can you lead a business like that?
How can-- so indeed, we didn't do so well financially and often had to have our grandparents help to make it.
I remember my parents often worrying, especially the later years, how they're going to pay the rent.
Later, my family opened another restaurant in a resort place not far from Miskolc.
And, my father used, used to carry the food out from Miskolc to Tapolca, was the name of this place.
And he didn't know how to ride the bike.
So he learned at age 46 or something how to ride the bike and carry the merchandise out there.
I don't know if I answered most of the things that I might want to talk about.
This was from 1928, when I was born, to about 1942 or so.
At 1942, when I finished the middle school, I could not go on to school anymore.
They would not take any Jews into the non-Jewish schools.
There were-- and they closed the only Jewish higher education, which was a teacher's training seminar.
They had to close that.
So I could not go to school, and I was devastated because I was such a good student.
And, so I decided to study languages, and I did that mostly on my own.
I studied German and Hebrew and English, so that I could do something.
I also started to learn a trade.
They said that would be helpful.
So I didn't get very far with that.
And then, I helped my parents in the business.
Now, in 1941 and '42 my uncles were all taken into forced labor battalions.
And, I remember how involved we were with them, trying to get them out.
There was one uncle who was called in so many times and we were able to rescue him through a lot of manipulation.
And, eventually we made him ill by injecting some kind of a virus in him.
And he became very ill, and that was the one that saved him the last time.
In order to not to be-- go, to be taken to the front.
They took these battalions to the Russian front and they worked right next to the Hungarian Army, but they were doing all the terrible work, and also, they were not fed well.
So, many of them had not returned after such an experience.
Also, my father's business became worse because we could not get food and we, we could not accommodate the other-- the people who came.
So that as well as our customers, they were largely traveling salesmen.
So their businesses folded, so they were no longer traveling.
So we didn't have the customers.
So often, we did not make our expenses.
Now, my brother, the last two years also, started to learn a trade, because we thought that maybe he could help out in the family earning something, some way of to making a living.
My younger brother couldn't do anything at that time.
He was just a little boy.
Well, you know Hungary was not occupied by the Nazis, by the Germans, until 1944.
And that was because the Regent of Hungary, whose name was Horthy, he collaborated with the Nazis.
And, it became, really, a Nazi-inclined government and with our own Nazi Fascist party, which was called the Arrow Cross party, and they were-- they often attacked the Jews without any reason.
Of course, by this time, the Jews could not attend any schools.
Certain businesses had to be closed, even before the Germans came in.
So that the Hungarians themselves, they, they had regulations and laws that prevented the normal life of the Jews.
But, we were still free to travel and to talk to people anywhere.
The first day was-- the first day was March 19, 1944, when the German Army marched into Hungary.
In spite of their negotiations, they decided to occupy and Eichmann arrived to Budapest.
And I was 15 and I wasn't really that much into politics or understanding what was going on.
So most of my experiences come from a perspective of a 15-year-old and 16-year-old, later.
When they came in, we were terrified.
And, my brother was a young man who loved football, or soccer, at that time it was called.
And, he traveled to Budapest regularly to see the soccer games.
And, on that Sunday-- March 19 was a Sunday-- he traveled to Budapest and he was arrested at the railroad station and did not return home, and we didn't know what happened to him.
My mother just returned that Sunday morning from visiting her parents in Edeleny.
And I, I felt terrified that day what was going to happen to us.
When the-- when the Germans marched into Hungary, everything that happened in other countries that took months or a year or years, it happened here very quickly.
So that only the following week, we were given the order to wear the yellow star and to give up all our businesses.
And, following that to go into the ghetto, which was in the same place, in the same Miskolc.
And, since I lived in that area, which was designated as the ghetto, people came to stay with us rather than us having to go somewhere else.
Now, of course, because our business was closed and because we no longer can employ non-Jews, the maids left.
And, they were too scared to come and, and visit us or to help us out.
We lived in a, in an apartment house where there were some non-Jews, and I don't know exactly what happened to them, whether they had to move out or not.
I don't remember that clearly.
There was a, a super, who was like a janitor, in the building and I think he-- they moved out, eventually, and somebody else moved into their apartment, but I'm not sure about that.
The 19th was the beginning of, of the reality that we could not face.

Because, even though we heard some rumors about Poland and Czechoslovakia and, and Yugoslavia-- I remember clearly when I heard that they took all these Jews to the Danube River, and they shot them, and they all fell into the river and they floated down the river, which became red from their blood.
I remember hearing that, but somehow I could not believe it.
And, the Hungarian Jews were especially so isolated.
They were kind of in between the more Western Jews and the Eastern Jews.
They did not speak Yiddish much, the Hungarian Jews, and they felt so patriotic.
I mean, my family had lived there for hundreds of years.
Although, my family from my mother's side comes from a very important family of the Horowitz-Margaretens with their genealogy going back all the way to 15th century.
My immediate family my, grandfathers, my parents, and so on, they all lived in Hungary for many, many years.
And, when I went to school, even though it was a Jewish school, we sang the national anthem every morning.
So, it was very hard for us to believe, probably similar to German Jews, that something can happen to us because we were such good Hungarians.
And it was-- it was like an unreality.
So, when we heard about the, the Czechoslovakians and the, the Polish, we said, well, it must be because they were not good patriots.
Maybe they had done something.
And, also, we did not hear anything what really happened.
We knew that they were shot and they were taken away, but nothing like Auschwitz or anything like that we heard about.
We also had some refugees from those countries, and they were illegally there in Hungary.
And, I remember the secrecy, how we tried to hide them and protect them.
And, often they went on during the night and escaped.
One night, we had a rabbi who lost 11 children who came to, to stay with us.
He lost his children few years back and he remarried in our back yard.
In the middle of the night they married him with a woman.
And then he went on.
I don't know where and I don't know what happened.
Well, first of all, let me tell you, we had a two-room apartment, plus our, our restaurant, plus kitchen and an entry hall, which was like another room.
And, we had about five families staying with us.
Those were staying with us all the time, as well as some families who came, or individuals who came, and then left.
The families who stayed with us were somewhat related to us, like distant relatives.
I think they had a choice who, who would come to live with us, and who we'd, we'd want to invite.
And, they brought in food with them so that we actually were better fed when they were there than before.
And, this family that moved in, they were well-to-do and they had five little children.
And, they brought butter and I remember seeing the mother giving bread and butter to the children.
How I would have liked to have a piece, but of course, I wouldn't ask for it.
And at this time, my father was still there, but my younger brother, my parents decided to send to Edeleny to be with my grandparents.
Because, he was very fragile and very young, and we decided that he would have more food there, so he left.
And, my father, eventually, was also called into the labor battalion and he also had to leave.
Now, this is a, a very emotional memory that I have of my father.
He was a man of pale, pale skin, always protected from the sun.
And, he was always gentle.
And, as he put his backpack on and I saw him saying goodbye, I had this feeling that I don't think I will ever see him again because, he did not look like he could survive any hardships.
And, indeed, I never saw him after he left.
My, my mother and I stayed in the ghetto and there were many air raids in the ghetto.
I suppose the air raids came from the Americans, but I'm not sure.
We had to go down to the cellar to the shelter when the air raids came.
And it was interesting, because I felt kind of happy being in the ghetto.
And, the reason, because I liked being with people.
I needed the comfort of having other people around me.
And, it felt like a community.
It felt like we were not alone.
It's very hard to appreciate how much that means to people not to be alone when something like this happens.
I had an interesting experience when I was in the, in the ghetto.
I-- There was a boy who came to stay with us and one night when there was an air raid we decided not to go down.
And, we watched how the bombs came down and he kissed me.
And that was the first time that I was ever kissed, and something that I cannot forget.
So, if you ask me what I felt, I don't-- I felt-- I was scared, I was afraid, but there were other feelings as well, kind of positive feelings.
I was also sad, and of course, we did not know what was happening to the rest of the family.
My grandparents, my-- so, that was always something that keeping us in constant tension, and so on.
Maybe-- is there anything else that you would like to know about the ghetto?
We stayed about two months in the ghetto.
It wasn't a long time.
It wasn't like-- you know, nothing compared to the Polish ghettos.
Then it was a much more quiet setting?
It was a much-- one thing, that I was taken out to work and I was able to bring in some food.
It wasn't a long time, but at least a few times I was able to go out to work.
Doing farming.
And, I was given-- and I remember, I was so hungry that I could eat up a whole loaf of bread at one time if I could get hold of that.
So, that was an exciting time for me, too.
